title: Provisioning Package Detected
description: Detects that one or more provisioning packages (PPKG) were present on the device during enrollment. PPKGs can inject local accounts, certificates, Wi-Fi/VPN profiles, apps (MSI/EXE) and scripts before or during Autopilot. They may be legitimate (bulk enrollment, OEM recovery) or a manipulation vector. This rule is ON by default so any PPKG is surfaced in every tenant. To suppress known-good packages, enable the template rule ANALYZE-SEC-006, set your allow-list, then disable this rule.
explanation:
A provisioning package (PPKG) artifact was detected on this device during enrollment.

- **Detected package:** `{{identity}}`

Provisioning packages can apply local accounts, certificates, Wi-Fi/VPN profiles, applications (MSI/EXE) and scripts. They are legitimate in bulk-enrollment and OEM-recovery scenarios, but a PPKG appearing on a device that is expected to be "pure Autopilot" can also indicate tampering.

Review the `provisioning_package_scan` event in this session for the full inventory (file names, registry package metadata such as ownerType/installTime, and best-effort content indicators).

**To stop alerting on known-good packages (2-step):** enable the template rule **ANALYZE-SEC-006 (Unexpected Provisioning Package)**, set its allow-list regex to match your approved vendor/recovery packages, then disable this rule. ANALYZE-SEC-006 then only fires for packages that are NOT on your allow-list.

remediation.title: Verify the provisioning package is expected
steps: Open the provisioning_package_scan event and review ppkgFiles + packages (packageId, name, ownerType, rank, installTime)
Confirm whether the package matches a known source: bulk-enrollment PPKG, OEM recovery package, or an internal provisioning workflow
Cross-check the device model/vendor — OEM recovery PPKGs are common and usually benign
If the package is unexpected for a pure-Autopilot device, treat it as a potential manipulation and investigate how it was applied (USB, Recovery\Customizations, image)
To allow known-good packages going forward, enable ANALYZE-SEC-006, set the allow-list, then disable this rule
